title: Oil Prices Surge Past $105 as Hormuz Crisis Squeezes Household Budgets Worldwide
description: Rising oil prices from the Strait of Hormuz crisis are driving inflation higher, pushing up costs for gas, groceries, and mortgage rates across the globe.
lede: Oil prices have surged past $105 per barrel as the Strait of Hormuz remains contested, triggering a chain reaction hitting household budgets worldwide — from gas pumps to grocery aisles to mortgage rates. Economists warn the ripple effects could push global inflation up by 1-2 percentage points if the crisis extends through Q2.

sections.heading: The Price Shock
paragraphs: Brent crude has climbed over 30% since the Iran conflict escalated, with the Strait of Hormuz — through which 20% of the world's oil flows — effectively disrupted. Trump's extended deadline to April 6 provides a narrow window, but markets are pricing in prolonged uncertainty.

For consumers, the math is brutal. Every $10 increase in oil prices translates to roughly 25 cents more per gallon at the pump. A family driving 1,000 miles per month could see fuel costs jump $50-75 monthly — and that's before the knock-on effects.

sections.heading: How It Hits Your Wallet
paragraphs: Oil prices don't just affect gas. They ripple through the entire economy. Shipping costs rise, pushing up grocery prices. Airlines add fuel surcharges. Heating bills climb. And when inflation rises, central banks respond by keeping interest rates elevated — which directly impacts mortgage rates and borrowing costs.

If you're planning a home purchase, the timing matters. Use an mortgage calculator to see how different rate scenarios affect your monthly payments. Even a 0.5% rate increase on a $400,000 mortgage adds roughly $120 per month.
For those tracking how inflation erodes purchasing power over time, an inflation calculator can show the real impact — $100 today could be worth $94-96 in a year if inflation stays elevated.

sections.heading: Investment Implications
paragraphs: Markets have reacted sharply. Energy stocks are up, but the broader S&P 500 has pulled back as higher oil prices threaten corporate margins and consumer spending. For long-term investors, the question is whether this is a buying opportunity or the start of a deeper correction.

History suggests oil shocks create short-term pain but rarely derail long-term market returns. If you're dollar-cost averaging into index funds, an investment calculator can help model different scenarios — including what happens if you keep investing through volatility versus trying to time the market.

sections.heading: What to Watch
paragraphs: The April 6 deadline is the immediate catalyst. If Iran reopens the strait, oil could drop $15-20 rapidly. If not, $120+ oil is plausible — and the economic pain deepens significantly.

Central bank meetings in April will signal whether rate cuts (previously expected in H2 2026) get pushed back further. For anyone with variable-rate debt, this is the time to run the numbers and understand your exposure.

faqs.q: How do oil prices affect inflation?
faqs.a: Oil prices impact nearly everything — transportation, manufacturing, heating, and agriculture all depend on fuel. A sustained $10/barrel increase typically adds 0.3-0.5% to headline inflation within 3-6 months.

faqs.q: Should I lock in a fixed mortgage rate now?
faqs.a: If oil prices keep rates elevated, locking in now could save money long-term. Use a mortgage calculator to compare fixed vs. variable scenarios based on your loan amount and timeline.

faqs.q: How do oil shocks affect the stock market?
faqs.a: Oil shocks typically cause short-term market declines as consumer spending tightens. However, historical data shows markets usually recover within 6-12 months as prices stabilize. Energy stocks tend to outperform during these periods.

faqs.q: What happens if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed?
faqs.a: Prolonged closure could push oil past $120/barrel, add 1-2% to global inflation, delay central bank rate cuts, and trigger recessions in oil-dependent developing economies.
